title: Heat Pump vs Traditional AC for Pomona Homes
meta: Compare heat pumps and traditional AC systems for Pomona and nearby SoCal homes facing hot dry summers and high electric bills.
hero_alt: HVAC technician checking a heat pump unit outside a Southern California home
body_html:
Understanding Your Cooling Needs in Pomona

Pomona homeowners often face hot dry summers with temperatures climbing well into the 90s and occasional Santa Ana winds that stir up dust. Traditional air conditioners have long been the go-to choice, but heat pumps are gaining attention because they can both cool and heat. The decision depends on your home's age, insulation, and how high your summer electric bills climb.

How a Traditional AC Works

A conventional AC pulls heat out of your indoor air and dumps it outside. It excels during peak summer heat but does nothing for the mild winter days common in Pomona, Ontario, and Chino. Many older homes in the area still rely on these units paired with separate gas furnaces, which adds to maintenance costs and complexity.

Pros of Traditional AC in SoCal

  • Strong cooling performance even when temperatures exceed 100 degrees near desert edges
  • Lower upfront cost for cooling-only systems
  • Simpler repairs when parts fail

Cons

  • No heating capability, so you need another system for cooler months
  • Higher electricity use during long heat waves
  • More wear from constant on-off cycling in dusty conditions

How Heat Pumps Differ

Heat pumps move heat rather than generate it. In summer they cool like an AC; in winter they pull warmth from outdoor air. For Pomona's moderate winters, this dual function often reduces overall energy use compared with running a separate furnace. Nearby cities like Rancho Cucamonga and Riverside see similar benefits when homes have decent insulation.

Pros of Heat Pumps

  • One system handles both heating and cooling
  • Lower operating costs when electricity rates favor efficient units
  • Quieter operation and fewer emissions than gas furnaces

Cons

  • Higher initial installation cost
  • Can lose efficiency below freezing, though rare in SoCal
  • Requires a qualified technician familiar with variable-speed models

Which System Fits Your Pomona Home

Start by checking your current electric bills and noting how often the system runs. Homes built before 1990 often benefit from a full evaluation of ductwork and insulation first. If you already have a working gas furnace in good shape, sticking with a traditional AC replacement may make sense. If your furnace is aging or you want to simplify to one unit, a heat pump deserves a closer look.

DIY steps include changing filters monthly during summer and keeping outdoor coils clear of dust and leaves. Anything involving refrigerant, electrical connections, or duct modifications should be left to a licensed pro. An honest load calculation will show whether your home's size and layout suit a heat pump's capacity.

Making the Right Call

Both options can keep Pomona homes comfortable when properly sized and maintained. Focus on your biggest pain points: high summer bills, winter heating needs, or simply wanting fewer appliances to service. A local technician can review your setup without pressure and explain realistic performance differences based on your address and usage patterns.

Contact BOMING Air Conditioning & Heating to schedule an assessment tailored to your property.

faq.q: Will a heat pump lower my electric bill in Pomona?
faq.a: It often does if your home is well insulated and you currently use both AC and gas heat. Savings vary by usage and rates.

faq.q: Can a heat pump handle Santa Ana wind conditions?
faq.a: Yes, modern units perform well in dry windy weather common around Pomona and Ontario when filters stay clean.

faq.q: Is replacing my AC with a heat pump worth it for older homes?
faq.a: Only after checking insulation and ducts. Many pre-1990 Pomona houses need those upgrades first for best results.

faq.q: How often should I service either system?
faq.a: Twice a year is ideal in SoCal to handle dust and heat stress, especially before summer.

topic: Heat pump vs. traditional AC: which is right for a SoCal home

title: Corona Homes: Leaky Ducts Driving Up Your AC Bills
meta: High summer bills in Corona? Leaky or dirty ducts waste cooling in SoCal heat. Learn real causes and fixes from a local HVAC pro.
hero_alt: HVAC technician checking attic ductwork for leaks and dust
body_html:
Living in Corona means dealing with long, hot summers where the AC runs almost nonstop. When ducts leak or fill with dust, that cooled air never reaches your rooms, forcing your system to work harder and driving up electric bills fast.

Why Duct Problems Show Up in Corona and Nearby Areas

Southern California’s dry heat, combined with Santa Ana winds, pulls fine dust through vents and into ductwork. Many homes in Corona, along with nearby Riverside and Ontario, were built decades ago with duct systems that have aged, settled, or developed gaps at joints. These issues are common in the region’s older housing stock.

How Leaks and Dirt Waste Cooling

Leaky ducts let conditioned air escape into attics or crawl spaces instead of your living areas. Dirty ducts restrict airflow, so the AC runs longer cycles to reach the thermostat setting. The result is higher energy use and uneven temperatures throughout the house.

Signs Your Ducts Need Attention

  • Rooms that stay warmer than others even when the AC is running
  • Visible dust buildup around vents
  • Higher-than-usual electric bills during peak summer months
  • Musty smells when the system turns on

Simple Checks You Can Do Yourself

Start by feeling for air coming from vents while the AC runs. Walk through the house and note any rooms that feel noticeably warmer. Look at exposed ducts in the attic or garage for obvious gaps or loose tape. Replace or clean your air filter monthly during heavy use seasons—this small step improves airflow immediately.

When to Call a Local HVAC Pro

If basic filter changes don’t improve comfort or bills stay high, it’s time for a professional duct inspection. Technicians use tools to measure airflow and locate hidden leaks that homeowners can’t see. Sealing and cleaning work is best left to pros because it often involves specialized equipment and proper sealing materials rated for HVAC use.

If you’re in Corona or surrounding cities like Pomona and Chino Hills and notice these patterns, contact BOMING Air Conditioning & Heating for an honest assessment of your duct system.

faq.q: How do I know if my ducts are leaking in Corona?
faq.a: Uneven room temperatures, high electric bills, and visible dust at vents are common clues. A pro can confirm with airflow testing.

faq.q: Can I seal my own ducts?
faq.a: Minor accessible gaps can sometimes be taped, but most leaks are hidden and require proper mastic or aerosol sealing by a technician.

faq.q: Does dirty ductwork affect AC performance?
faq.a: Yes. Built-up dust restricts airflow, making the system run longer and use more electricity to cool the same space.

faq.q: How often should ducts be inspected in SoCal?
faq.a: Every 3–5 years is reasonable, especially in dusty areas near the desert or after major wind events.

topic: Leaky or dirty ductwork: how it wastes cooling and raises bills
